mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Fiji on?
mapQuestions.answer: Oceania (the Pacific). Fiji is in the South Pacific Ocean, about three-quarters of the way from Hawaii to New Zealand 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Fiji?
mapQuestions.answer: Suva, on the south-east coast of the main island Viti Levu. About 175,000 people live in Suva. It is one of the biggest cities in the South Pacific.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Fiji speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Three official languages: Fijian (also called iTaukei), Fiji Hindi (spoken by the descendants of Indian workers brought during British colonial times), and English.

mapQuestions.question: How many islands does Fiji have?
mapQuestions.answer: 333 islands in total! About a third of them are inhabited. The two biggest are Viti Levu (where most people live) and Vanua Levu.

mapQuestions.question: Where does the date change in Fiji?
mapQuestions.answer: The International Date Line passes right next to Fiji. The country chose to be on the WEST side, so it is one of the first places in the world to see each new day (just after Kiribati and Tonga).

foods.name: Kokoda
foods.description: Fijian raw-fish "ceviche": white fish (often mahi-mahi) cured in lime juice, then mixed with coconut milk, chilli, onion and tomato. Served in a hollow coconut shell.

foods.name: Dalo (taro)
foods.description: A starchy root vegetable, boiled or roasted, eaten at every meal. The Pacific staple, with a slightly nutty taste.

foods.name: Lovo
foods.description: Whole pig, chicken, fish, dalo and sweet potato wrapped in banana leaves and cooked underground over hot stones for hours. The Fijian feast for special occasions.

foods.name: Fiji curry
foods.description: Inherited from Fiji's Indian community: rich coconut curry with goat, chicken or vegetables, eaten with roti bread or rice. One of the most loved meals in Fiji.

foods.name: Coconut milk + cassava chips
foods.description: Fresh coconut cracked open and drunk straight, or cassava (a starchy root) deep-fried into crispy chips, a classic Fijian beach snack.

foods.name: Vudi vakasoso
foods.description: Cooking bananas (plantain) boiled in sweet coconut milk with brown sugar. A traditional Fijian dessert.

scavengerItems.label: A vendor cracking open a fresh coconut to drink
A turquoise lagoon with white sand and palm trees
A village chief's bure (traditional thatched-roof house)
Bright reef fish around a coral head (Fiji has some of the world's best snorkelling)
A hibiscus flower tucked behind someone's ear
A "lali" drum (a hollow wooden log drum, banged to call the village together)
Coconut palms leaning over a beach
Everyone shouting "Bula!" as you walk past
A sea turtle swimming over a coral reef
A reef shark gliding past while snorkelling (mostly harmless to people)
A waterfall in the rainforest interior of Viti Levu

geoMatches.description: The main island, biggest in Fiji. Home to Suva (capital), Nadi (where you fly into), and the famous Coral Coast.
A long chain of small idyllic islands off the west coast, white-sand beaches and turquoise water. The location of the film "The Blue Lagoon".
A closer cluster of tiny island resorts, easy to reach by boat from Nadi. The "Survivor" reality TV show has filmed here repeatedly.
Fiji's second-biggest island. Quieter, less developed, with rainforest interior and the small Fijian-Indian capital Labasa.
The "Garden Island", a volcanic island famous for waterfalls (Bouma), rainforest hikes, and the international date line running through it.
The capital, on Viti Levu's south-east coast. The biggest South Pacific city outside Australia and New Zealand. Markets, museums, government buildings.

senses.smell: Coconut oil on people's skin, sweet frangipani and hibiscus flowers, salty sea breeze, woodsmoke from village fires, the rich earthy smell of cooked dalo…
senses.hear: "Bula!" greetings everywhere, the boom of waves on the reef, ukulele and guitar songs around a village fire, "meke" traditional drumming and singing, palm leaves rustling in the trade wind…
senses.taste: Fresh coconut water, lime-cured kokoda fish, rich Fiji-Indian goat curry, smoky lovo pork, sweet vudi vakasoso bananas in coconut cream…
senses.feel: Warm Pacific water (around 26°C all year), white-sand beaches, the soft heat of the sun, the surprise cold of a rainforest waterfall, the smooth carved wood of a kava bowl…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is a kava ceremony?
stories.body: Kava (called "yaqona" in Fijian) is a drink made from the pounded root of a Pacific pepper plant, mixed with water and strained. It looks like muddy water and tastes earthy. In Fijian villages, sharing a "bowl of kava" is the most important social and ceremonial act: when you arrive in a village, you present the chief with a "sevusevu" (a gift of dried kava root), and you all sit cross-legged on woven mats around a big carved wooden bowl while clapping and drinking in a clear order of seniority. Kava itself is mildly relaxing (it numbs your lips a little). Kids in a village ceremony are usually allowed to sit and clap with everyone but not drink. The ritual matters more than the drink.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole community sits in a circle, in order of seniority, to welcome a stranger. How would that change how visitors behave?

stories.question: Why does Fiji have an Indian community?
stories.body: Between 1879 and 1916, the British colonial government brought around 60,000 workers from India to Fiji to work on sugar-cane plantations under "indenture" contracts (basically forced labour with a 5-year minimum). When the contracts ended, most of those workers stayed. Their descendants ("Indo-Fijians") today make up about a third of Fiji's population. Indian and Fijian cultures have lived side by side for over 100 years now, sometimes peacefully, sometimes with tensions. Indo-Fijian curry, roti bread and Fiji Hindi (a unique creole language) are all woven through everyday Fijian life. The mix is one of the most distinctive things about Fiji compared to other Pacific islands.

stories.question: What is the International Date Line?
stories.body: The International Date Line is an imaginary line, roughly along the 180th meridian, where each new calendar day starts. Cross it heading west, you skip ahead a day. Cross it heading east, you go back a day. The line zigzags through the Pacific to keep individual island groups all on the same date. Fiji decided to be on the WEST side, meaning Fiji is one of the first places in the world to see each new day (after Kiribati and Tonga). The line actually runs through the island of Taveuni in eastern Fiji: you can stand on a hillside there with one foot in "today" and one in "yesterday".

stories.question: Why are Fijians some of the friendliest people on Earth?
stories.body: Fiji has one of the strongest reputations for friendliness of any country anywhere. Walk down any village path and EVERY person will shout "Bula!" with a huge smile. This is rooted in the traditional Fijian concept of "vanua", which is a kind of community responsibility for one's village, family and visitors. It is also rooted in "lomani" (love and care) and "veikauwaitaki" (kindness). Children are raised by the whole village together. Newcomers are received as guests, not as strangers. There is also a sense of relaxed time (called "Fiji time"), where rushing isn't the goal. Fiji can change how you think about how communities are supposed to work.

stories.question: How healthy are Fiji's coral reefs?
stories.body: Fiji has some of the best-preserved coral reefs in the world. The "Great Astrolabe Reef" off Kadavu is one of the largest barrier reefs anywhere. The famous "Rainbow Reef" off Taveuni is one of the few places where soft corals grow in vivid pinks, oranges and purples. Climate change and rising sea temperatures threaten Fijian reefs the same way they threaten reefs everywhere, but Fijian communities have been very early adopters of protecting "tabu" zones (areas where fishing is banned) to let reefs recover. Snorkelling over a healthy Fijian reef is an experience you might not get many more chances to have in your life.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Fiji so far?
Have you said "bula" to a stranger?
Beach, reef, village, rainforest, which has felt the most "Fiji"?
Have you seen a coconut being cracked open? Or done it yourself?
Snorkel a reef and tell me ONE thing that looked unreal.
Fiji has a strong reputation as one of the friendliest countries on Earth. Why might that be?

wordSearch.hint: Fijian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Coral reef fish
animals.description: Hundreds of species around the Mamanuca and Yasawa islands. Bright butterflyfish, parrotfish, sergeant majors.

animals.name: Green sea turtle
animals.description: Glide through the reefs and rest on shallow sandbanks. Protected and rebounding well.

animals.name: Blacktip reef shark
animals.description: Common on shallow reefs, usually harmless to people. Distinctive black-tipped dorsal fin breaking the surface.

animals.name: Collared lory
animals.description: A bright red-and-green parrot found only in Fiji.

animals.name: Coconut crab
animals.description: The biggest land arthropod on Earth (up to 9 lbs). Climbs coconut trees to drop them. Mostly seen at night.

animals.name: Mantis shrimp
animals.description: A small reef shrimp that punches with the speed of a bullet. Among the fastest movements in the animal kingdom.

animals.name: Humpback whale
animals.description: Migrates through Fijian waters July to October, breaching and tail-slapping near the islands.
